sections.title: Parts of Speech
rows: run, walk, do, be, shall, eat, love
you, me, I, them, yourself, anything

sections.title: Noun Types
rows: Refers to a class of nouns; general category
Refers to a specific/particular noun; always capitalized
Can be counted; can be pluralized

sections.title: Zero Plural (no change)
sections.content: cod, series, deer, odds, sheep, wheat, elite, regalia, species, headquarters, means, swine

sections.title: Always Singular (no plural form)
sections.content: advice, aid, ammunition, documentation, evidence, homework, implementation, jargon, jewelry, learning, smoke, work

sections.title: Always Plural (no singular form)
sections.content: assets, lyrics, scissors, manners, minutes (of a meeting), shorts, belongings, spirits (alcoholic drinks), statistics (facts), clothes, pants, thanks, credentials, proceedings, ties, eyeglasses, riches, remains

sections.title: Verb Tenses
rows: I walk to school every day.
Action that began and ended before the present
Action that will happen in the future
My driver will pick me up at 3 PM.
We are preparing for our final thesis defense.
Action in progress at a certain time in the past
My husband was working in the bodega last night.
Ongoing action continuing into the future
I will be dancing until the sun rises.
Action completed at an indefinite time in the past
The school has increased the tuition fee.
Past event that happened before another past event
When I arrived home, he had already called.
Action completed by a specified time in the future
We will have finished this job by April.
Action that began but has not finished yet
The value of the PHP has been falling.
Continuing action in the past overtaken by another past action
I had been waiting for your output before I got sick.
Action happening now that will still be happening at a future moment
At 8 PM, Divine will have been studying for ten hours straight.

sections.title: Subject-Verb Agreement Rules
rows: The child runs towards the ice cream truck.
The children run towards the ice cream truck.
Singular indefinite pronouns (each, either, neither, everyone, nobody, etc.) → -s form verb
Everybody wants to attend the concert.
Plural indefinite pronouns (both, few, many, several) → base form verb
Only a few keep lions as pets.
Subjects joined by 'and' → base form verb
A telephone line and an internet connection are sold together.
or/nor, either…or, neither…nor → verb agrees with the nearer subject
Neither the teacher nor the students are ready.
Collective nouns → singular (one unit) or plural (individuals)
The group has decided to go on a trip.
A number of girls favor the new makeup set.
The number of girls has increased.
Expressions of money, time, or measurement → -s form verb
Ten pesos is not enough for a full meal.
Nouns plural in form but singular in meaning → -s form verb
The news was disheartening. (also: mumps, mathematics, economics, physics)
The + adjective used as noun → base form verb
The wicked are not allowed in heaven.

sections.title: Types of Conjunctions
items: Coordinating Conjunctions — Join elements of equal rank. FANBOYS: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so.
Correlative Conjunctions — Paired conjunctions: not only…but also, both…and, either…or, neither…nor, not…but.
Subordinating Conjunctions — Join a subordinate clause to a main clause: after, although, as, because, before, if, since, unless, until, when, where, while.
Conjunctive Adverbs — Act like conjunctions; clauses they join remain independent: however, consequently, furthermore, moreover, nevertheless, therefore, thus.

sections.title: Common Sentence Errors
items: Run-on Sentence: Two or more sentences not separated by proper punctuation or conjunction. Fix with a period, semicolon, comma + coordinating conjunction, or subordinating conjunction.
Sentence Fragment: Incomplete sentence with missing subject or predicate. ❌ 'Andy studied hard. Passed all his tests.' ✅ 'Andy studied hard and passed all his tests.'
Parallel Structure: Elements performing the same purpose must be in the same form. ❌ 'I like running, biking, and to take a long hike.' ✅ 'I like running, biking, and taking a long hike.'
Misplaced Modifier: Modifier placed too far from the word it modifies. ❌ 'Elijah cut himself while shaving badly.' ✅ 'Elijah cut himself badly while shaving.'
Dangling Modifier: The word being modified is not in the sentence. ❌ 'To pass the exam, review lessons must be taken seriously.' ✅ 'To pass the exam, one must take review lessons seriously.'

sections.title: Modal Verb Guidelines
items: shall vs. should — Use shall with I and we when asking for advice or making a suggestion: 'Shall we dance?' Use should for other persons.
will vs. would — Use will or would for requests (would is more formal). Use will for predictions.
can vs. may — Use can to ask permission under general rules or custom. Use may to ask permission from a specific person.
may vs. might — May = serious or immediate possibility. Might = remote possibility.
Modal verbs do not occur with each other. ❌ 'I will be going to submit my report.' ✅ 'I will be submitting my report.'
